title: Getting to Heaven Is a Community Project
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Getting to Heaven is a Community Project __ Hebrews 12_12-17 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Life of the Christian Is Movement
subpoints: Progressive sanctification: we cannot be the same today as we were yesterday
Westminster Shorter Catechism: sanctification is God's free grace renewing us more and more after the image of God
Suffering was threatening this congregation's movement toward Christ
The Holy Spirit reminds them that suffering comes from a good, loving, and merciful God and is designed to propel us toward Christ

outline.title: Command 1: Strengthen the Hanging Hands and Feeble Knees (v. 12)
subpoints: Based on Isaiah 35:3-4 — 'strengthen the weak hands and make firm the feeble knees'
The image of drooping hands and paralyzed knees describes severe spiritual discouragement
The command is plural: the rest of us are to come alongside and help the discouraged person stand
How to strengthen the weak: point them to Christ — to the cross, and to his coming again
You will not arrive at the end by yourself — corporate responsibility for one another's faith is stressed throughout Hebrews

outline.title: Command 2: Make Straight Paths for Your Feet (v. 13)
subpoints: Based on Proverbs 4:26 — 'ponder the path of your feet and let your ways be established'
The image: go ahead of the struggling runner and smooth the track so the lame may not be further injured but healed
This language is normally used of servants preparing the way for a king — here the 'king' is the spiritually injured
How to smooth the path: remind people of who they are in Christ — an heir, a child of God, an object of his infinite favor
The greatest encouragement to keep moving is the gospel itself, not trying harder

outline.title: Command 3: Pursue Peace with All People and Holiness (v. 14)
subpoints: Based on Psalm 34:14 — 'depart from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it'
Peace includes enemies — we are to love and pray for those who do us harm (Romans 12:17-19)
The pursuit is diligent and constant — present imperative: keep on pursuing
This pursuit is a mark of the sons of God: 'Blessed are the peacemakers' (Matthew 5:9)
Peace is not the ultimate goal — the glory of God is; peace must be pursued according to God's terms, not at the expense of holiness

outline.title: The Danger of Failing to Obey: Three Warnings (vv. 15-17)
subpoints: Warning 1 (v. 15a): falling short of the grace of God — if you do not live in fellowship, you risk not arriving at heaven
Warning 2 (v. 15b): a root of bitterness springing up — isolation from the community produces deep, all-encompassing bitterness
Warning 3 (vv. 16-17): ungodliness, illustrated by Esau who sold his birthright for a single meal and later found no place for repentance
No brother, no sister left behind — it is a corporate calling to ensure we all arrive together

summary:
In this sermon on Hebrews 12:12-17, Pastor Tito Lyro argues that arriving at heaven is not a solo project — it is a communal undertaking. The passage is built on three Old Testament allusions (Isaiah 35, Proverbs 4, and Psalm 34) and issues three commands to the church: strengthen the spiritually discouraged, make the path smooth for those who cannot finish the race on their own, and pursue peace and holiness with all people.

The vivid imagery of drooping hands and paralyzed knees describes believers so beaten down by suffering and discouragement that they cannot continue running toward Christ. The answer, says the Spirit, is not addressed to the discouraged person alone — it is a plural command directed to the entire community. The healthy runners are to come alongside the fallen runner and help them stand. The way to do this is the same way Isaiah did it: point them to God, to the cross, and to the coming of Christ.

The second command builds on the same image: the stronger members are to go ahead of the struggling ones, clearing the track — filling the potholes and bringing down the hills — so that the spiritually lame are not further injured but are given the chance to heal and keep moving. The greatest encouragement for anyone to keep moving is not willpower but the gospel: knowing who you are in Christ as an heir, a beloved child of God, and the object of his infinite, unearned favor.

The third command is to pursue peace with all people — including enemies — without sacrificing holiness. This pursuit is a defining mark of the children of God. The sermon closes with three warnings about what happens when believers fail to live in community: they fall short of grace, grow bitter at the root, and become like Esau — who despised the gifts of God and later found no way back. The call is to a 'no one left behind' Christianity: grabbing the person next to you and running together to the end.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 12:12-17

Great, but would you be standing for a moment longer, guys, as we read God's Word. Today is a Sunday where we're going to be turning to Hebrews chapter 12 as a stand-alone message for today. Hebrews chapter 12, starting in verse 12 and reading through verse 17. You notice it starts with a "therefore," which means it's connected to what goes before. So later today, please take a look at that.

So Hebrews chapter 12, starting in verse 12. This is the word of our Lord.

> Therefore, strengthen the hands which hang down, and the feeble knees, and make straight paths for your feet, so that what is lame may not be dislocated, but rather be healed. Pursue peace with all people, and holiness, without which no one will see the Lord. Looking carefully lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up cause trouble, and by this many become defiled; lest there be any fornicator or profane person like Esau, who for one morsel of food sold his birthright. For you know that afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no place for repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together.

Lord, your word is powerful in your hands. It changes people, it changes us. We pray that your Spirit will be present with us and that he will be working mightily through the proclamation of your word, that our hearts may be closer to Christ. We are asking in his name. Amen. Please be seated.

## The Life of the Christian Is Movement

The life of the Christian — that dynamic life — is a life of movement. Everything in his life and everything in her life is moving. It is moving toward Christ. And if that is the case, if life is always moving, if you are always moving toward Christ, then change is the norm in our lives. We cannot be the same today as we were yesterday, and hopefully tomorrow we are not going to be the same as we are today. That is what we call progressive sanctification. We are progressing toward glory, becoming more and more like the Lord Jesus Christ. The Westminster Shorter Catechism describes this doctrine as follows: *Sanctification is the work of God's free grace, whereby we are renewed in the whole man after the image of God, and are enabled more and more to die unto sin and live unto righteousness.*

As we look at Hebrews 12, we see that suffering in the life of this congregation was threatening their movement toward Christ. Suffering was keeping them from growing, from being sanctified, from progressing toward the Lord Jesus Christ. So the Holy Spirit reminds them that even suffering in life comes from the hand of a good, loving, and merciful God. Chapter 12:5-6 says that. And the Holy Spirit tells us in verse 11 that suffering is designed to help us be conformed to Jesus Christ, even though it is not pleasant. *Nobody is rejoicing while you are being chastised by the Lord, but if you learn from it, it will produce the fruit of righteousness.*

So because suffering is designed to propel us toward Christ, the Holy Spirit says to this church: don't just sit there. Get up and move toward Christ. The exhortations of verses 12 through 17 are built on three passages from the Old Testament that this church would have been very familiar with. Three allusions, three commands from the Old Testament.

- Verse 12 alludes to **Isaiah 35:3-4**
- Verse 13 refers to **Proverbs 4:26**
- Verse 14 refers to **Psalm 34:14**

The Holy Spirit repackages these three passages and gives them to us as three commands: in verse 12, *strengthen*; in verse 13, *make straight*; and in verse 14, *pursue*.

When the Christian stops moving toward Christ in these ways — when a Christian stops strengthening, making straight, and pursuing — he or she will fall short of the grace of God, bitterness will grow at the core of who they are, and ungodliness will result. This is of utmost importance, because it is in doing what the Holy Spirit tells us here that we are going to arrive together in glory.

## Command 1: Strengthen the Hanging Hands and Feeble Knees

> *Therefore, strengthen the hands which hang down and the feeble knees.* (Hebrews 12:12)

The word *strengthen* here is related to the prefix *ortho* — as in orthodontist, who straightens teeth; or orthopedic doctor, who straightens bones. The idea is of strengthening by making straight. It is often used in the Gospels to describe miracles where a crippled person was made straight again — for example in Luke 13:11-13, where a woman had been bent over for years and Jesus says, "Woman, you are freed from your infirmity," and immediately she was made straight.

Here in Hebrews, though, it is not referring to a physical making-straight but a spiritual one. The image of hands hanging down the sides of the body is a vivid picture of discouragement — the person walking slumped over, drooping. And the feeble knees are not just weak — the word describes paralysis. These are knees that have ceased to work. The same word is used in Luke 5 for the paralyzed man whose four friends lowered him through the roof.

Picture a marathon runner, halfway through the race, who collapses and goes limp — unable to move forward. That is the image being portrayed here of this brother, this sister: so spiritually discouraged that they are just lying there, not moving forward.

I remember vividly watching the marathon finals of the 1984 Olympics — the first time a women's marathon was run in the Olympics. In the final laps at the LA Coliseum, the last surviving runner could barely move. She was leaning one way, then the other. Organizers rushed to help her; she pushed them away, because once they helped her, she would be disqualified. She literally fell across the finish line. That limpness, that inability to keep moving because there is nothing left within yourself — that is the description here. Not in a physical sense, but in the spiritual sense.

I guarantee that some of you are there right now — limp, discouraged, bent over, not feeling like there is one ounce of grace in your heart to keep on pursuing Christ.

**What is the solution?** The rest of us come alongside and help that person stand straight. Notice: the Spirit is not speaking to the one who is discouraged. The Spirit is talking to all of us, commanding us to come alongside and strengthen the weak. The other runners are to come up and strengthen the fallen runner. You will not arrive at the end by yourself. That is the main point here.

This corporate responsibility for each other's faith and perseverance to the end is stressed throughout the book of Hebrews:

> *Exhort one another daily while it is called today, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin.* (Hebrews 3:13)

> *Let us fear, lest a promise remaining of entering his rest, any of you seem to have come short of it.* (Hebrews 4:1)

> *Let us therefore be diligent to enter that rest, lest anyone fall according to the same example of disobedience.* (Hebrews 4:11)

> *Let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is the manner of some, but exhorting one another, and so much the more as you see the day approaching.* (Hebrews 10:24-25)

Do you see, brothers and sisters, how much we need each other? Some of you may get annoyed with the emphasis your elders place on being in church, on not letting anything get in the way. You might think, "They just want to control us." No, brothers and sisters — we want you to arrive at the end. And if you forsake the Lord's worship on the Lord's Day, that is the first step toward veering from your goal and no longer running, looking at the Lord Jesus Christ.

**How do we strengthen the weak?** Isaiah 35 gives us the answer: *Say to those who are fearful-hearted, "Be strong, do not fear! Behold, your God will come with vengeance, with the recompense of God; He will come and save you."* You point them to God — particularly to the coming of Jesus Christ.

Do you want to encourage one another? Point each other to the cross — how it has vindicated us, how the Father took vengeance on our sins there, how we have been saved from Satan, from self, and from the wrath of God. We can persevere through suffering because he persevered through suffering. And we point them to the fact that Christ is coming again. This life is not all there is. Jesus is waiting for us at the end.

## Command 2: Make Straight Paths for Your Feet

> *And make straight paths for your feet, so that what is lame may not be dislocated, but rather be healed.* (Hebrews 12:13)

Now the image shifts. Instead of stronger runners coming alongside the collapsed runner in the middle of the road, they are going ahead of the struggling runner — the handicapped runner — making sure the track is clear. *Straight* here is not the opposite of curved. It is the opposite of bumpy and full of divots. The idea is of making the way plain — filling the holes, leveling the hills — so that the lame can walk and get to the end. This is to be done so that those who are spiritually injured will not be injured more by the track and will have the opportunity to heal as they continue toward Christ.

Interestingly, this language in the rest of Scripture is only used of servants who make the path smooth for kings. And yet here, the one whose path is being made straight is the injured one — the one who cannot finish on their own.

How do we make the track plain? How did John the Baptist make the way plain for Jesus? By proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom — pointing to the King. Jesus is the one who heals the lame. He is the one who fills the potholes of our spiritual lives.

Brothers and sisters, the greatest encouragement to keep moving is the gospel and who we are in Christ. Not by trying harder. Not by looking within ourselves. But knowing who you are in Jesus Christ: you are an heir of all things, you are a child of the creator of the universe, you are the one whom the Father loves with the same love that he loves his Son, you are the object of God's infinite favor. That's who you are. And when you run knowing that, you'll get to the end.

## Command 3: Pursue Peace with All People and Holiness

> *Pursue peace with all people, and holiness, without which no one will see the Lord.* (Hebrews 12:14)

The third command is to pursue peace. *Pursue* — that is a present imperative: keep on pursuing peace. And notice: it is a categorical command to pursue peace with *all people*. Who are all people? All people — including our enemies. Remember what Jesus instructs us to do to our enemies: to love them and to pray for them. They are your enemies because they think of you that way — not because you think of them that way.

Paul says in Romans 12:17-19: *Repay no one evil for evil... Beloved, do not avenge yourselves, but rather give place to wrath; for it is written, "Vengeance is Mine, I will repay, says the Lord."*

What is peace? Peace is the absence of strife and contention. The absence of animosity and bitterness. The absence of anger. The presence of goodwill. The ability to forgive and to ask for forgiveness. A lack of focus on self and a presence of focus on others. And do you notice how that only exists in relationships? The peace the Bible speaks of only exists in relationships.

Paul says in Romans 12:18: *If it is possible, as much as depends on you, live peaceably with all men.* Do you get that? *As much as it depends on you.* You do not have the excuse to allow relationships in your life to go unresolved. You cannot sit on your hands with a self-righteous attitude, waiting for the other person to come to you. As much as within yourself, you must pursue peace.

This pursuit is a characteristic of the sons and daughters of God. Remember the beatitude: Matthew 5:9 says, *Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.* If you are not a pursuer of peace — if your life is marked by broken relationships because of you — you have no confidence to call yourself a child of God, because *blessed are the peacemakers for they shall be called sons of God.*

Now, this pursuit of peace is not, however, to be done at the expense of holiness. *Pursue peace with all people and holiness* — singular holiness — *without which no one will see the Lord.* Peace is not the ultimate goal. The glory of God is. Peace is a means to declare God's glory. Therefore it must be pursued according to God's terms. You cannot be a peace-faker who looks like a peacemaker. Peace is pursued according to God's Word, which also leads to holiness.

## Three Warnings: What Happens When We Fail (vv. 15-17)

The Holy Spirit also warns us that it is spiritually dangerous not to obey these three commands. Verses 15-17 give three *lest* warnings — three things that will likely happen to you if you do not pursue these things.

**First:** You are going to grow bitter. If you do not value community, if you are not investing in the brethren, bitterness will grow. And notice: the text speaks of a *root* of bitterness — not a twig. The root is the base of everything; it is deep and pervasive. Everything in that person's life will flow from bitterness.

**Second:** Ungodliness will mark your life. The example given is Esau, who sold his birthright for a pot of stew — a bowl of hot cereal. Can you imagine? He didn't value the gifts of God. And later he wanted it back, and there was no chance. That is the kind of person who doesn't value the gifts God gives through his people.

**Third:** You may miss the grace of God. This doesn't mean a true believer can lose their salvation. But the means by which God makes a true believer arrive at the end are the people around them. If your life lacks grace, if your life is marked by ungodliness, if you have a bitter heart — watch out. You may be missing heaven itself because you are missing Christ in this life.

## The Goal: No Brother, No Sister Left Behind

Remember in the Bush administration, the educational plan was "No Child Left Behind" — the audacious goal of every kid being able to read a paragraph by third grade.

This passage says: no brother, no sister left behind. You are going to grab the person next to you, and you are going to make sure each other makes it to heaven. Because that is what God has appointed as the means for us to run our race.

You know that, right? If you work out by yourself, it is easy to quit. But if someone else is there — even the fear of embarrassing yourself in front of them — it keeps you going. There is that Christian encouragement that we need as we run as well. And we look, point each other to Jesus, keep on facing Jesus together, and arrive together.

So strengthen the weak, pursue peace with all people, make the way plain for those who on their own cannot do that. And together we run, looking to Jesus, and arrive together. And in heaven together we hear the great words: *Well done, well done, my good and faithful servant.*

May that be the words that we all hear as we stare at Jesus face to face.

## Closing Prayer

Glorious God, we thank you that you are a good God who enables us to run this race and who gives us each other to encourage one another to the end. Help us to feel that. Help us to be in each other's lives. Let us allow people into our lives, that we might run the race faithfully to the end. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
